Apparently Windows GUI apps don't define `__stdout__`: https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.__stdout__

For the time being you can work around the issue like this:
```python
import sys
sys.__stdout__ = sys.stdout
import mt940
```

* The SqlAlchemy breaks the zero dependency. You can try to use sqlite3 directly or at least wrap it in a try…except ImportError clause.

But it comes down to the same restriction; you cannot add/remove what you've just removed/added. 

Let's talk about SBFS for ease of explaining.

The only unconstrained operation you can have is Step 1, i.e. you can remove a feature from the current feature set. Now, the inclusion operation comes with a certain type of "memory" as in, it remembers the feature you've just removed. That's for both Step 2 and Step 3. 

I think what confused you is that he said X_{k+1} instead of X_{k-1} which in a sense shows an insertion but it's actually the removal, the element at that "time step" which was up to that point kept in memory reached the constraint and as such it will be removed. I find SFFS is much easier to understand (due to k+1 being intuitive) and plus, SBFS is just the mirror version of it.

Tell me if that makes sense.

Hi,
the connection must be bound because the ObjectDef must read the schema to build the correct AttrDef list. Also the REUSABLE client_strategy is quite complex to use and is intended for multiple threading access to the LDAP server. If you don't need this you can use the RESTARTABLE or the SYNC client_strategy.

so in your code just add ``conn.bind()`` before defining obj_def. The 'fooMethod' object_class must exist in the schema for automatically read the schema and build the correct AttrDef list.

Face detection isn't part of the LFW evaluation.  Importantly, the images in the LFW challenge are already face detections. Each image is a capture of one face that is centered squarely in the middle.   LFW is an evaluation of the face verification part only.  

So don't throw away images that the HOG detector, in the example program, incidentally doesn't find.  That's not part of LFW.  Although, as an aside, dlib includes a CNN face detector that's more accurate and can find all the faces in LFW, but is slower to execute.

It's not related to powerpc.  But the error you are getting doesn't make sense.  It's saying that this code:

```
std::array<unsigned int,4> cpuid(int) { return std::array<unsigned int,4>{}; }
inline bool cpu_has_avx_instructions()    { return 0!=(cpuid(1)[2]&(1<<28)); }
```

is the issue.  But that code is fine.  Maybe your compiler is just broken.  Right, read the error:

```
error: cannot convert 'bool' to '__vector(4) __bool int' in return
inline bool cpu_has_avx_instructions()    { return 0!=(cpuid(1)[2]&(1<<28)); }
```
That makes no sense since that function doesn't return a vector of bools.  It's just returning a bool.


Anyway, maybe there is a workaround.  You need to find some minimal piece of code that generates the error and then you will be able to find some workaround based on that.  But the underlying issue seems to be some weird compiler bug.

@jusplainmike It should not go into [`hangups.auth.RefreshTokenCache.set`](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/3a1d8932ed6d8542397eda9139b66ee4a96a883c/hangups/auth.py#L130) but into the file that it writes to, which is likely `~/.cache/hangups/refresh_token.txt`.

```python
from os.path import join

print(join(AppDirs('hangups', 'hangups').user_cache_dir, 'refresh_token.txt'))
```

Or provide your own path with `$ hangups --token-path /my/path/to/refresh_token.txt`

How do you expect lib to pass a variable into your function if there is no argument provided? Use an error handler and check the error type instead.

A "generic" error handler would be like
```py
@bot.event
async def on_command_error(error, ctx):
    # you can compare error here
```

A "per-command" error handler would be like
```py
@test.error
async def test_error(error, ctx): 
    ...
```

Short answer: yes, it will do the right thing in production and so there's no need to have different production and development settings.

The slightly more complicated answer is that it won't switch itself off as such, because what it's doing is forcing `runserver` into production mode (with regard to static files) all the time. `contrib.staticfiles` comes with its own wrapper around `runserver` which takes over static file handling when in `DEBUG` mode. All `whitenoise.runserver_nostatic` does is disable this behaviour and use the standard, production-mode `runserver` regardless of whether `DEBUG` is enabled or not.

Hope that makes some kind of sense!

Is your Django app running on the root of the domain, or under the `/production` path?

When running at the domain root everything works out of the box, but if you're running under a particular path you need to configure [WHITENOISE_STATIC_PREFIX](http://whitenoise.evans.io/en/stable/django.html#WHITENOISE_STATIC_PREFIX) explicity e.g:
```
STATIC_URL = '/production/static/'
WHITENOISE_STATIC_PREFIX = '/static/'
```

> How do I style a line chart?

If the docs don't get you started, then read the code, which is what I just did. Looks like you can subclass your Line chart and merge your styles into the stylesheet like so:

```
class StyledLine(svg.charts.line.Line):
    def get_stylesheet(self):
        ss = super(StyledLine, self).get_stylesheet()
        my_styles = load_my_styles() # returns a cssutils style sheet.
        ss.add(my_styles)
        return ss
```

Then use that class to generate your line chart.

I acknowledge that the style customization interface is clumsy, which is why this ticket exists. This project is very generous with accepting user contributions, so do please if you can think of a better way, please share it and we'll get it into the next release.

Hi @southmeadows!

I think what you are referring to is a child row... just add the class defined in the [`cssChildRow` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#csschildrow) , which is `"tablesorter-childRow"` by default, to attach the child row to the parent and keep those rows together. The documentation has some example HTML and even that row in the documentation is a child row.

There are two examples included in the documentation as well:
* [basic child rows](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-child-rows.html).
* [child rows with filter widget](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-child-rows-filtered.html).

If you are referring to nesting tables, then I would recommend using a custom parser to set which part of the nested table data to use for sorting.

I've been thinking about doing that - have a module which centralizes the code used to gather column data; but I think I just need to add a non-parsed value along with a parsed value for each cell in the cache. If that was done, the filter widget would not need to extract the text again and probably speed it up even more.

There is a problem with centralizing text extraction though... because each widget would need to do something a little different to a column. For example:

* The formatter widget cycles through every row in the table just once. While processing a row, it figures out which columns need to me modified. This is much more efficient than cycling through every row for the first modified column, then every row for the second modified column, etc.
* The output widget is a little different in that it actually takes `colspan` and `rowspan` within the `tbody` into account.
* The filter select option code only grabs the text for one column. If there is more than one column that needs a select, it cycles through each one independently -maybe this could be made more efficient by following the same pattern as the other widgets.

I'll have to look into this more when I have more time, as it will require changes to numerous widgets.

Doesn't the already existing [two-digit-year parser](https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/blob/master/js/parsers/parser-date-two-digit-year.js) not work for you? See [the demo here](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-parsers-dates.html).

It supports three formats ( "mmddyy", "ddmmyy" and "yymmdd" ) and it looks for a two digit year within a specified range (default is 50 years), so if a table were to list a year as "99", the parser would interpret it as "1999" and not "2099".

Honestly, I would prefer not to add a new parser directly to the core plugin unless it were essential; but if you wanted to submit a parser in a separate file, I would be more open to including it in the project.

@davidparsson this is because to avoid arbitrary code injection, the library uses subprocess.Popen and passes a list according to the following spec:

```
program, *args = [a1,a2,...]
```

the OS then resolves the `program` executable, e.g. looking in `$PATH`, etc. and tries to execute the program with the name `program`. In this case, `vim -v` is not a valid program as it tries to execute `vim\ -v`, which is not found on your system.

If you're using the latest codebase it should not be an issue, but I'm pinging @fmoo here, we should not be using `args.split(' ')` to avoid "dumb" behaviour, instead use `shlex` library to handle shell arguments properly.

1. Setup CefWidget as usual inside layout
2. Create a hidden window with QWindow() and pass its window handle to CEF when creating browser
3. After browser is embedded in the hidden window, create container with a call to QWidget.createWindowContainer(window=hidden_window, parent=CefWidget)
4. Replace CefWidget in the layout with the container

Stranger here (never tried plumbum but interested in programmatic access to virtualenvs), but it seems it would be threadsafe if you keep a modified environment as a local variable and pass it to `Popen` (or `run`) as the `env` argument, without touching the process-global environment. With no teardown (other than the ordinary)) it no longer makes sense as a context manager.

```python
def virtualenv_runner(venv_dir):
    virtualenv_path = '{}:{}'.format(venv_dir, os.environ['PATH'])
    virtual_environ = dict(os.environ)
    virtual_environ['PATH'] = virtualenv_path
    if 'PYTHONHOME' in virtual_environ:
        del virtual_environ['PYTHONHOME']

    virtualenv_python = os.path.join(venv_dir, 'bin', 'python')

    def _virtualenv_run(args, **kwargs):
        if args[0] == 'python':
            args[0] = virtualenv_python
        run(args, env=virtual_environ, **kwargs)

    return _virtualenv_run


myvirtualenv_run = virtualenv_runner('/path/to/virtualenv')
myvirtualenv_run(['python', '-c', 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'])
```

Hello,

The targets added by the `scripts/kconfig/Makefile` patch are needed to pick up environment variables exported from the Kbuild Makefiles, because those can be referenced in the Kconfig files (via `option env="SOME_ENV_VAR"`).

In practice, I've only ever seen three environment variables being referenced: `ARCH`, `SRCARCH`, and `KERNELVERSION`.

`ARCH` is the architecture (x86, sparc, etc.), `SRCARCH` a subdirectory in `arch/` where files for the ARCH are stored. Usually, `SRCARCH` will match `ARCH`. Search for "Additional ARCH settings" in the top-level Makefile to see cases where it doesn't.

`KERNELVERSION` is a version string. I've never seen anything depend on its exact value, but you can set it to the output of `make kernelversion` to be safe.

So, to run Kconfiglib on e.g. `ARCH=x86` without the Makefile targets, you can do this:

    $ ARCH=x86 SRCARCH=x86 KERNELVERSION=`make kernelversion` python
    >> import kconfiglib
    >> c = kconfiglib.Config() # defaults to loading "Kconfig"

That should match what you get with `scriptconfig` for any kernel version I've seen.

As for the thing you're trying to do, the approach I would use would be to iterate over the symbols that can be changed (`is_modifiable()`), changing them in strategic ways, and seeing if that alters the visibility of the "target" symbol. That way you could probably work out some kind of minimal set.

Note that this might get tricky in some cases since a symbol might depend on `A || B` or `!A` for example. If the only operator was `&&`, it would be easier to define what it means for one symbol to "require" another.

I'm currently working on a non-backwards-compatible 2.0 version of Kconfiglib by the way, with a much cleaned-up API ("less is more"). That one will also expose all the expressions in a clean way. It's safe to peek at them in the current version too if needed though, even though they're "internal".

Cheers,
Ulf

Hm, maybe we need to launch `tar` or `zip` in a subprocess, and stream its data to the other side. We wouldn't know how large the zipfile will be ahead of time, though, so the best we could do to advise the receiver about what they're about to get would be to run `du` over the source directory. We'd also need to make sure to not allow a truncated transfer to look like a complete one (the current code knows exactly how many bytes to expect; the new implementation would need to send a special distinct "stop" message at the end).

Well, there is a `--run-perf=check` option that runs performance tests with `--benchmark-disable` ([code](https://github.com/eisensheng/pytest-catchlog/pull/40/files#diff-9a3b6a42d3e9b1879cee16f75d55ddefR78)), though this is mostly for sanity checking reasons, i.e. to ensure these tests just compile, run and pass (this is what we also use for Travis builds).

The more general idea is to be able to:
  - benchmark certain typical scenarios (see test cases in tests/perf/bench)
  - measure how pytest invocation options affect that (plugin disabled / no reporting / no capturing, etc. - [code](https://github.com/eisensheng/pytest-catchlog/pull/40/files#diff-9a3b6a42d3e9b1879cee16f75d55ddef))
  - and then plot everything to estimate dynamics from change to change.

And it is possible to plot not just benchmark results of a single test case, but also combine results of several test cases using an expression. For instance, by default `--perf-graph` would plot the results of `(caplog - stub)` expression, which is a shorthand for `(test_caplog_fixture - test_fixture_stub)`. That is, take the result of benchmarking `test_caplog_fixture` (the cost of preparing our fixture + inevitable overhead of the pytest itself) and subtract the result of `test_fixture_stub` (just the pytest overhead) to get the "pure" cost of making our `caplog` fixture.

Yep, `PrettyContext` is fully immutable except for the `visited` attribute, which is a mutable `set` tracking visited values to prevent recursion. Having that mutable is just a performance optimization. So `PrettyContext.set` is indeed misleading; perhaps `replace` would be Pythonic as used in the immutable APIs for `datetime.datetime` and `namedtuple._replace`.

`pretty_repr` for filling in `__repr__` methods was added in 0.8.0 https://prettyprinter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html#prettyprinter.pretty_repr PR #6 implements support for the `_repr_pretty_` interface used by `IPython.lib.pretty`, which provides the following output now: Recursive calls to `p.pretty` in `_repr_pretty_` implementations are delegated to the normal `prettyprinter`, which is able to produce colorized output seen in the above screenshot.

If you want to get your plugins running immediately, you can update their code to be compatible with this version of redactor.

For each plugin you need to update the Object that the plugin is adding the method to. So for example, replace the following:

```
(function($)
{
	$.Redactor.prototype.fontcolor = function()
```

with:

```
if (!RedactorPlugins) var RedactorPlugins = {};

(function($)
{
	RedactorPlugins.fontcolor = function()
```

You can do this with all of their plugins and they will work as expected.

class Loss(mx.gluon.Block):
    def forward(self, score, target):
        bsz = score.shape[0]
        mag = (score**2).sum(1).broadcast_to((bsz, bsz))
        sim = mx.nd.dot(score, score.T)
        dist = (mag + mag.T - 2 * sim)
        dist = mx.nd.Activation(dist, act_type="relu") + 1E-9
        dist = mx.nd.sqrt(dist)
        return mx.nd.mean(dist)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import numpy as np
    np.random.seed(123)

    score = np.random.uniform(0, 1, (20, 3))
    target = np.random.choice(range(3), 20)

    loss = Loss()

    score = mx.nd.array(score)
    target = mx.nd.array(target)
    score.attach_grad()
    with mx.autograd.record():
        l = loss(score, target)
    l.backward()
    print(score.grad)
```

I'm ambivalent. It seems more sensible to me for the instance to be the first arg, since it makes bound methods and unbound methods have the same signature. But I'm sympathetic with @takluyver's concerns about inserting an argument at the front.

If we are thinking about new ways to do things, I would actually prefer to add new ways to register listeners that *don't* involve inspecting the callback, rather than keeping the counter growing indefinitely on positional arguments.

>  But the reason we did the repo split was the enable
higher level components (ipywidgets) to move quickly and break things.

Yes, I don't mean that this applies to everything, but traitlets is probably where we should be the *most* conservative about breakage. Part of the reasoning of the split is that we have components at widely varying levels of stability—some very stable, some very unstable. Widgets are *highly* unstable and experimental, and I view as free to break all the things, while traitlets *was* quite stable. Most of the other packages lie somewhere in-between.

> Traitlets is challenging because it is at the very bottom, but also used
extensively by ipywidgets in advanced ways that require its evolution (not
breakage necessarily though).

Yup, traitlets is a tricky one because of that. I'm AOK with new APIs, and as the work here has demonstrated so far, we can make new APIs without unnecessarily breaking the old ones. They can be deprecated and stay deprecated for quite some time. Deprecating doesn't need to put a ticking clock on removal, either—we don't have to remove deprecated APIs until maintaining them becomes a problem.

> Even though we don't want to be forced to do lock-step releases of all packages, that doesn't mean we *can't*, when needed. 

Quite true, but hopefully it will never (or rarely) be needed. I doubt we will ever be in a position to need a release of traitlets that would break the stable version of any other project, for instance. I would expect sensible deprecation cycles to allow releases of other packages to have been made naturally in the meantime.

> I think I am mostly reacting possibly over-generalized idea of "no API breakage for 2 years". We still have a lot of things to break in parts of our code base...

I didn't mean to imply that I was trying to dictate a strict rule, especially one that would apply to every one of our repos the same, sorry about that. I was just trying to convey the idea that we should be conservative about breaking widely used low-level APIs on our most stable, core packages. In general, I think we should do our best to do more of exactly what's been done here: make new APIs adjacent to old ones and deprecate more gently, with the deprecation time (time matters much more than version numbers for deprecations) taking into account how much code needs to be updated to use the new APIs. I think at least a year for `_foo_changed` to keep working is a reasonable ballpark estimate for something like that. I didn't mean for the statement to be stronger than that, though.

The mention of Atom I made in the original post was merely meant to mention other implementations of this type of things, which may give some inspiration. Making APIs a bit more similar API is only *a plus* in that people who are familiar with one are not lost with the other, but I did not mean actual compatibility or replacement.

- Regarding the reservations of @takluyver w.r.t. API changes, the two first items that I proposed in the original post are backward compatible and not very disruptive:

    - deprecating the use of bare trait type types:

    ```Python
    class Foo(HasTraits):
        bar = Int    # deprecated
        baz = Int()  # ok
    ```

    It simplifies a lot the metaclass and will remove a lot of idiosyncrasies of the current implementation, especially the top-level meta class. Hence, I guess this is probably not the part you would disagree on.

    -  separating the metadata from keyword argument through a deprecation of the old behavior is also a rather minor change, which will clarify the API IMO.

    Overall, these two items are not more disruptive than the other things that happened in traitlets lately such as not allowing `None` by default, changing the mechanism for default value initializaiton etc... I think that traitlets are already a bit cleaner now than a year ago.

    - The more disruptive changes regarding having a nicer replacement for `on_trait_change` could clearly be more disruptive, and we should probably all agree before we move forward with the actual implementation.

I would rather we do keep separate flavors of the issue described here. I specifically stressed out the possibility to have the signature be customized in terms of adding additional components that participate in the process. 

Content-MD5 being described as "meaningless" in the GET requests does not disallow it to be present. After all, empty value still gives you a hash albeit deterministic.

The use case described just brings up some additional custom contract to the process. If say Alice and Bob decide to include the custom header in the process of signing url requests, than Charlie being not a part of that contract will have an additional step to be able to get a pre-signed url working for him.

There are lots of scenarios that might be exampled here, without stressing out a specific header.

In the meantime, other SDKs like AWS Java SDK and a "hand-coded" boto library does support that feature without violating any strict contract from the AWS Signing Interface.

@jamesls What do you think would be the best format for tracking these types of comparisons?  I don't necessarily think it makes sense to put these in-code (as the difference are mostly removals). My workflow on this has been less about "this is what requests does" and more about "this is what botocore expects" (passing all the tests). In general my answer to "Why is this different from requests?" will be "because botocore doesn't use it, afaik".

At a really high level we're basically taking two things from requests and re-implementing the subset that botocore excepts. These two pieces are:
* requests model objects that represent a request & response (`AWSRequest`, `AWSPreparedRequest`, `AWSResponse`)
* Requests adapter class for `urllib3`

As for the `Urllib3Session` which parallels to the `HTTPAdapter` class in requests there shouldn't be much difference. The big difference is:
* Our vendored version of requests implemented chunked encoding for request bodies. Newer versions of urllib3 support this by setting `chunked=True` when calling `urlopen`, so we don't need it in our http session class. Additionally, I've not enabled this flag because afaik we never use chunked encoding when making requests.

The model objects do have a lot of changes as I basically gutted them to have next to no logic and only implemented the things that we relied on requests for. This turns out to be not a whole lot of functionality as botocore is already producing / preparing generally well structured requests before handing them off to requests. The behavior that I've found we needed is:
* Converting the modeled params dictionary to the query string
* Determining content length of the body
* Converting the headers to a case-insensitive dictionary
The biggest differences here are url preparation and determining the content length of the body. Requests parses the url does some validations and conversions on it and reconstructs it. From what I've gathered this process basically just fixes any issues the url may have and I've never ever seen it actually change a url after it was done. I verified this by running all of the tests asserting that the url before and after are the same. As for determining content-length of the body I tried to keep it simple and implement the logic that we document (it's `bytes` or a `file-like`).

The current version of nix/nixpy now behaves like this:

```python
nix_file = nix.File.open('foo.h5', nix.FileMode.Overwrite)
nix_file = nix.File.open('foo.h5', nix.FileMode.Overwrite)
```

This throws a `RuntimeError` because an opened file is opened again with truncate mode. I think this is the correct behavior. Reopen a file with other open modes works, which is also OK.

To reopen a file with truncate mode it has to be closed properly. This can be done with `close`, or by assigning `None`:

```python
file = nix.File.open('foo.h5', nix.FileMode.Overwrite)
file = None
file = nix.File.open('foo.h5', nix.FileMode.Overwrite)
```

```python
file = nix.File.open('foo.h5', nix.FileMode.Overwrite)
file.close()
file = nix.File.open('foo.h5', nix.FileMode.Overwrite)
```

Therefore I suggest to close this issue as well as #78.

@techalchemy Yeah, I think this is a case where attempting to re-use the existing installation-focused resolvers is actually the root of the current problem, since the requirements for platform-independent lock file resolution are *just* different enough that we've ended up trying to fit a square peg into a hole with slightly rounded corners. If Python didn't support environment markers I believe the existing approach would work, but as things stand, the platform-dependent nature of dependency installation is leaking out into the lockfile generation.

For cases like `pywin32`, where `setup.py dist_info` fails on non-Windows systems, I think the best we're going to be able to do is:

- check PyPI for transitive dependency info, and use it if available
- if PyPI doesn't have anything, then fail the lock operation by default (but have an opt-in toggle like `--skip-incompatible` that converts any such errors to warnings instead)

And we'd then advise publishers to either 1) make their metadata extraction work cross-platform; or 2) upload wheel files so PyPI can extract and publish the metadata via the JSON API

@uranusjr I originally also thought running the lock file generation inside the venv would be enough, but then I later realised it still isn't enough to solve the problem:

* if you abide by environment markers, but use a Py3 venv to generate the lock file, you'll still miss locking Py2-only dependencies (i.e. running in the venv just inverts the current "lockfile was generated with the wrong Python version" problem, it doesn't fix it)
* generating the lockfile on Linux or Mac OS X would still miss Windows-specific dependencies and vice-versa
(Fix will land in `11.11.0`)

Actually that wouldn't be accurate. The protocol is a negotiation between the server and client, but ultimately the client decides the protocol to use to start the app. The log message being discussed here is logging the client's final decision, not what the server advertised. For example suppose we add protocol 1.1 which adds a 'PING' message where the server replies 'PONG' and use this for a heartbeat. This is backwards compatible. The server would advertise 1.1, but until we modify the client code, we'd still be using 1.0 to bring it up. So it would be incorrect to say we're bringing up the snippet using protocol 1.1 until we actually adjust the client code to make use of the new protocol features. 1.0 would be the right thing to log here.

You might consider doing something like using qgrid inside of a layout widget which places some sort of ipywidgets control above qgrid, which can show whatever information you want about the selected row.

As of qgrid 1.0.3 you can attach event handlers for almost any interaction that occurs in qgrid.  In the case you're interested in the relevant event would be `selection_changed`, so listening for that event would look like this:
```
def handle_selection_changed(event, qgrid_widget)
    # update the ipywidgets control above the grid
    pass

qgrid.on('selection_changed', handle_selection_changed)
``` 

See the [API docs](https://qgrid.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#qgrid.QgridWidget.on) the [readme](https://github.com/quantopian/qgrid#events-api) or the [events notebook](https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/quantopian/qgrid-notebooks/master?filepath=events.ipynb) for more information.

So let me explain, how H2O's R works - H2O exposes REST API which exposes capabilities provided by 
Java API.
On the top of REST API, we built several clients including R/Python/Flow UI.

So if you import H2O's R package into your R session ( `library(h2o)` - it is available via CRAN), 
we override few functions, but to make it work, you have to connect your R client to an existing H2O 
cluster via `client <- h2o.init(ip=..., port=...)`
 From this point you can use all capabilities provided by h2o package 
(http://h2o-release.s3.amazonaws.com/h2o/rel-slater/5/docs-website/h2o-r/h2o_package.pdf).

The trick is that if you run H2O on top of Spark (as Sparkling Water), you have access to the same 
REST API.
So you can connect to it from your local machine, but even more you can connect to it also from 
sparkR code (in theory, but it is one of our goals for next months).

Does it make sense?

Michal

We already have `bootstrap` section in the configuration file, where we can put some initdb arguments, add lines to pg_hba.conf, create users and populate `/config` key in DCS.

So we can just extend this section:
```yaml
bootstrap:
  method: my_bootstrap_method  # if there is no `method` defined - use initdb

  my_bootstrap_method:
    command: /my_bootstrap_command.sh
    restore_command: '/my_restore_command.sh %f %p'
    recovery_target:
      action: promote  # should we enforce it?
#      name:
#      time:
#      xid:
#      inclusive:
#      timeline: latest
```

It looks like you have misconfigured your Etcd, and therefore, Patroni cannot proceed.

See the page at
https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/v2/clustering.html
as an example on how to configure Etcd with a static list of members.

If you need to add a new member to the cluster, the process is really 2-step. See 
https://github.com/etcd-io/etcd/blob/master/Documentation/op-guide/runtime-configuration.md#add-a-new-member

From the examination of your logs it seems the primary issue is that you haven't cleaned up your previous etcd installation before starting the new one (the first node being started `2067e570df39ae8a`  tries to send requests to another node `e27f59d4e2dd4c9e` ). 
```
Aug 28 01:27:29 s2n etcd[11349]: 2018/08/28 01:27:29 raft: 2067e570df39ae8a [logterm: 2, index: 912198] sent vote request to e27f59d4e2dd4c9e at term 20019
Aug 28 01:27:29 s2n etcd[11349]: 2018/08/28 01:27:29 etcdserver: publish error: etcdserver: request timed out
```

There are other potential issues as well (advertising localhost, not following the proper procedure when adding a node), please, look at the links above and try to follow them.

It is crucial to understand how Etcd works before relying on it. You may want to look at how RAFT (the algorithm behind Etcd distributed consensus decision making) works, i.e. look at the official docs: https://coreos.com/etcd/

A working etcd (as confirmed by `etcdctl cluster-health` and `etcdctl member-list` is a pre-requisite for running Patroni. Without it, Patroni will fail immediately after trying to connect to Etcd, as demonstrated in your logs.

Last, but not least, running your Etcd cluster with 2 nodes is ok for the test environment, but never do it in production, since there is no resiliency in this setup. Etcd requires a majority of nodes to continue  to continue accepting writes, and the majority of 2 is 2. Consider running at least 3 nodes on 3 different servers (running Etcd and PostgreSQL on the same server is also not recommended), that way you can loose one Etcd node and still keep your cluster running.

As a sidenote, posting your logs in docx might make them unreadable to some people. Consider always using plain-text format for the logs and output you share with others.

A Python API for accessing a flat parameters file could be like: ```python def function(self, simulation, period): value = simulation.legislation_at(period.start, 'isf.decote.base') # or value = simulation.legislation_at(period.start, 'isf', 'decote.base') ``` The notation `simulation.legislation_at(period.start).isf.decote.base` is not useful anymore since we don't need to store sub-nodes in variable anymore, like: ```python def function(self, simulation, period): decote = simulation.legislation_at(period.start).isf.decote # [...] some code base = decote.base # [...] some code ``` Also, having names in dot-notation allows to *grep* those names, which is useful when we see them in the formula source.

If I may summarise to see if I understand the crux of the issue:

 - you wish to capture additional details of successes to an external test tracking system ?

Sure, you can do that with something like your patch, and I'm ok with something like that being added - though we should take the time to consider the impact on async tests, where futures are a big part of the API.

I'm not sure if you are aware though, but tearDown is not guaranteed to run - if setUp fails, tearDown does not run. Only cleanUps are guaranteed to run. The reasons for this are fundamentally backwards compatibility, though cleanUps can provide stronger guarantees in part due to Python's cooperative subclassing behaviour (can't guarantee that someone remembers to upcall for tearDown).

So I wonder if it would be useful perhaps to decouple the two considerations you have:
 - capture success metadata
 - capture to an external tracking system

For the former, addDetails will capture arbitrary (and in principle arbitrarily-sized, though I'm sure we have not got it streaming and/or/buffering everywhere as yet) attachments on both success and failure (the default UI just doesn't show anything if the test case succeeds). For the latter a custom StreamResult  object - such as ``subunit.run`` uses - would allow you to gather structured (e.g. via JSON) and unstructured (e.g. log messages) to an external tracking system. You can use a Y adapter to both capture to such a system and show a text UI to folk running the tests interactively. One advantage of doing it this way is that your test code would not be responsible for capturing to the external system, and so would neither forget to do so, nor need awkward ways to control it externally. OpenStack uses subunit2sql as part of its test aggregation and reporting infrastructure, and thats a very similar use case.

Anyhow, I don't intend to try and force you to solve the problem the way I would ;) - so lets focus on the review at hand.

I think this sounds reasonable. One adjustment would be necessary:

Currently the main thread is the one actually _doing_ the work by calling `wait`. Because rust futures are (mostly) "pull based", if we switched from `wait` to `poll` there, no thread would actually be working on the future. So I think that we'd additionally need to move the root futures into a `CpuPool` (or some other Future executor). Then those threads would drive them to completion, and rather than a future directly held by the `Graph` (a `Shared` future), `Scheduler::execute` would be holding a `CpuFuture`. Dropping it would _probably_ cancel the work on the pool ... but this isn't precisely clear, because `CpuPool` might only be able to cancel before the work has started.

I actually think we should not be setting the `AWS_DEFAULT_PROFILE`, which is what the current implementation does as well. In general environment variables are the user's space. We should not be manipulating them silently as it can lead to unexpected results.

The best option in general would be to set the profile on the session. However, we do not have access to that as the command is ran in a subprocess. Currently testing a workaround, it looks stupid but the template does deploy!

Two lambda functions, same code or different up to you!

* If it is an incoming request, the user already knows the API URL.
* Otherwise, if it is an outgoing request triggered by an AWS event for example, the outgoing function has the URL in the environment variable that you can send over to your "first time" user maybe via e-mail or a webhook that points back to the "incoming" lambda function.

```
  Incoming:
    Type: 'AWS::Serverless::Function'
    Properties:
      FunctionName: Incoming
      Description: Incoming
      Runtime: nodejs8.10
      Handler: index.handler
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 30
      AutoPublishAlias: prod
      Events:
        Trigger:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /
            Method: post

  Outgoing:
    Type: 'AWS::Serverless::Function'
    Properties:
      FunctionName: Outgoing
      Description: Outgoing
      Runtime: nodejs8.10
      Handler: index.handler
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 30
      AutoPublishAlias: prod
      Environment:
        Variables:
          URL: !Sub "https://${ServerlessRestApi}.execute-api.${AWS::Region}.amazonaws.com/Prod/"

```

The findings are stored along with the configuration in the JSON datastructure stored in `scout2-report/inc-awsconfig/aws_config-<profile_name>.js`. The first line of the file breaks the JSON compability, it is used to declare the variable in the report's JS.

A simple way to get the data out of the report would be like that:

```
tail scout2-report/inc-awsconfig/aws_config-l01cd3v-scout2.js -n +2 | jq '.services[].findings[]'
```

This command outputs the file's contents (but skip the first line) then passes the valid JSON payload to jq and prints all the findings. Then you'd want do do some additional filtering on the value of the level (danger) and check whether the list of items is empty or not.

It's a matter of interpretation of what "in" means.

```
import copy
l = [ (0,0), (1,1) ]
e = copy.deepcopy(l[0])
e is l[0]  # returns True
e == l[0] # returns True
e in l # returns True

l = [ [0,0], [1,1] ]
e = copy.deepcopy(l[0])
e is l[0] # returns False
e == l[0] # returns True
e in l # returns True
```

I think the difference in behavior is caused by tuples being immutable, while lists are mutable. 
As a side-effect it neatly demonstrates that apparently the "in" operator uses == instead of "is" for comparisons. That seems to be consistent with the note-in-stream behavior you considered wrong. 
At least it seems consistent with the rest of Python.

As discussed on IRC, giving apps access to the host's docker daemon opens up an attacker's ability to ruin your day simply by killing all containers running on that host, or worse yet running arbitrary commands inside your other containers. We do this to allow users who wish to run Deis as a hosting platform make this possible without exposing too much of the underlying software layers involved.

The better solution to this problem is to treat the daemon like a [backing service](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/using_deis/config-application/#attach-to-backing-services), which drone supports by using `deis config:set DOCKER_HOST=tcp://192.168.0.1:2357`. Flags that make sense like `-m` and `-c` are exposed through commands like `deis limits:set`, but we do this in a proper manner to sanitize input from the user.

I would personally pin the root cause of this issue as a lack of fallback logic on SynchronousQueue. If you look in a code it's a very rudimentary "while True:" loop that will go forever until the sender has successfully sent all its data. If the web call blocks or if the internet connection drops, this loop will hang your program. I realize that it is intended to be a synchronous queue so blocking is a natural consequence, but this code should be more resilient to cases where the remote service never responds and fallback or try again later.

For others who are affected by this issue I would recommend using an AsynchronousQueue in your telemetry client constructor:
instrumentation = TelemetryClient(instrumentation_key, TelemetryChannel(queue = AsynchronousQueue(AsynchronousSender())))

@voith @pipermerriam   i don't think you want to treat ws provider analogous to httpprovider, which means reuse and requires some additional management at the request manager level. otherwise you may end up not only blocking a lot of ports on the clients-die but possibly bring the ws server on the node to grinding halt. not likely but plausible especially in a multi-user per node situation.

let's say you have a (global) deque, called ws_registry, possibly pre-seeded with, say, five ws connections at startup (as part of the Web3(WSProvider(...))  init), and the modified make_request procedure pseudo-looks like so: 
```pseudo
if provider_type is websocket
    if ws available
        ws = ws_registry.popleft() 
    else
        ws = make_ws_connection
    execute request using ws
    ws_registry.pushleft(ws)
```
you ought to get away with ws clients based on a slightly modified websocket echo client [example](https://github.com/websocket-client/websocket-client/blob/046cb908d14647070e0cd04333461c7627156f10/examples/echoapp_client.py) which is seems reasonably clean.

add ws_registry.pushleft(ws) to the on_open and ws_registry.remove(ws) to the on_close, i think you're mostly covered. it's still far from ideal but imo you end up reusing connections getting the real ws benefits.  
if you then add a background task that sweeps ws_registry to keep-alive at least k, e.g., five, connections, remove the ones the server closed due to inactivity, and, if you want to be fancy, limit the "idle" number of connections on ws_registry to, say, 3*k, using something like [ws.close() for ws in ws_registry[-1:-j]] you should be ok ... i think.

>Solving the conda-forge environment requires a lot of work, and I constantly see times of two to five minutes for a simple conda install.
>
>I will be trying the new master branch and see whether it helps.

The repository index parsing is something that only takes at most a few seconds. The "two to five minutes" you are seeing is time spent by the dependency solver. In almost all cases this happens because R packages are involved in dependency resolution. There are many duplicates between the `defaults` and `conda-forge` channels for those packages. Additionally, those packages have many builds (for different R versions). If you constrain the version of the `r-base` package, you might get better results. See https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/5536#issuecomment-386935346 for more information.

For the record, I really prefer the behavior of not showing the packages. Often I remove an environment that has dozens of packages. I like to check the name of the environment one more time in the confirmation. With the behavior before this PR, that was trivial - it was the only line asking for confirmation. With the 4.3 behavior, (and the behavior after this PR), I have to scroll up past the package listing and locate the one line where it said the environment it was removing to double-check.

For me, things would be better if the name of the environment I was removing was immediately above the confirmation prompt. When removing an environment, I'm thinking on the environment level, not the package level.

I've been considering incorporating this and submitting a PR for quite a while now, but couldn't settle on a useful representation of how the parsed MD-tag would be available. A dictionary over each position with FromBase>ToBase mutation? Only a list of those positions that include mutations and which these are?

As it's something I use quite often on a day to day basis, I settled on pushing my regions of interest through a separate pileup-creation step, which I can then use to read out the percentage of FromBase>ToBase mutations in that area. Loading the pileup into pandas this becomes a trivial task:
```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import pysam

def GetPileup(bam_file, region=None, start_pos=None, pileup_len=100, reference=None):
    region_str = region + ':' + str(start_pos) + '-' + str(start_pos + pileup_len)
    pileup_sam = pysam.mpileup(bam_file, '-r', region_str, '-f', reference, '-d', '20000000')

    parsed_pileup = [dict(zip(['ref_id', 'ref_pos', 'ref_base', 'coverage', 'read_base'],
                     x.split()[:-1])) for x in pileup_sam]

    return pd.DataFrame(parsed_pileup)

def ParsePileup(pileup_df):
    alphabet = 'ACGT'
    for nuc in alphabet:
        pileup_df.loc[:, nuc] = np.where(pileup_df.ref_base.str.upper() != nuc, pileup_df.read_base.str.count(nuc), np.nan)
    for f_base in alphabet:
        for t_base in alphabet:
            if f_base != t_base:
                pileup_df.loc[:, f_base + '>' + t_base] = np.where(pileup_df.ref_base.str.upper() == f_base, pileup_df[t_base] / pileup_df.coverage * 100, np.nan)
    return pileup_df


pileup = GetPileup(bam_file, region, start_pos, ref_file_str)
pileup = ParsePileup(pileup)
```
This should give you a pd.DataFrame object, with a column for each X>Y mutation and it's percentage, and does so relatively quickly, too. However, keep in mind, that the region supplied for the pileup results in ALL reads covering this region, regardless if they start at `start_pos` or only overlap there.

The parameters are used to limit the operations according to whether the resource has been modified or not. 
None of the batch commands take these parameters into account upon `--dryrun`.

`--dryrun` will simply list all blobs under **consideration** for deletion/upload.
This will always be a superset of the actual blobs that would be deleted/uploaded.
Only once the actual command is run will the properties be applied on a blob by blob basis.

We've run into a problem with this - it breaks the ability to cache the querysets and return them from the `get_queryList()` method.

Code like this used to work:

```
def get_queryList(self):
    querylist = cache.get(CACHE_KEY)
    if querylist is None:
        # Calculate querylist and cache here...
        ....
    return querylist
```

And mean that subsequent calls to the API would not result in a new DB query.
But now this doesn't work because each queryset is forced to be re-evalulated by `query.all()`

Equivalent code works fine in DRF because the re-evaluation is forced in `get_queryset`, so if `get_queryset` is overridden it's assumed the user knows what they're doing.

So the right fix is to move this to the `get_queryList()` function. I'll prepare a PR.

1. `git rebase -i HEAD~30`, in the editor, drop all the commits except for

2. [optional] If you don't have the upstream remote url in `.git/config`, you have to add with
```
[remote "upstream"]
	url = git://github.com/projectmesa/mesa.git
	fetch = +refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*
```
3. `git pull --rebase upstream master` (instead of merge)
This step updates the local branch `remove-unused-class-params` to the latest master and apply `f2b737e681df6f82fef6378bcdf31dceeee8b6a7` on it.
4. `git push -f` overwrites the `origin:remove-unused-class-params`

Hello!

There is indeed a reason for not treating async def test functions automatically as asyncio tests: it's that there are other Python async frameworks based on coroutines, like https://github.com/dabeaz/curio and https://github.com/python-trio/trio, and this would make it impossible to support tests for these other frameworks in the same code base (since curio tests would be async def coroutines too).

So the current approach is opt-in. The current approach to reducing boilerplate is using pytestmark at the module or class level (see here: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-asyncio#pytestmarkasyncio).

This still requires a little bit of boilerplate at the top of the file. I wonder if we could do something with a custom pytest option. For example, by modifying pytest.ini or setup.cfg you could opt into treating all async def tests as pytest-asyncio tests.

Well, you can write simple to moderate asyncio applications using just a single loop in the main thread, and asyncio itself supports it with the asyncio.get_event_loop function. So people relatively new to asyncio are likely to just write code that makes asyncio use the global loop, and those are the people that we should support out of the box.

Now, we can talk about ways to document this and make it easier to test without a global loop. For example, we could have a command line parameter that changes the default from False to True. Then you could just add this setting to your pytest.ini.

Awesome, can you submit a PR with your chances?  Some initial feedback based on the linked bug:

> * only include gss.h as the other header files don't exist - this can easily be IFDEFed (I just hacked it)

Yep, the three header files are already designed to conditionalize logic like that (they currently do it for OSX strangeness).  You'd just need to modify setup.py to populate a constant to indicate that we need `gss.h` if `gssapi.h`, etc are missing on Windows.

> * gss_mech_krb5 is not defined in those header files, I used python struct.pack to generate the value.

There's a method on the `OID` class itself that should do that for you (`from_int_seq` if you have numbers, or use the `elements` argument to the constructor if you have bytes).

However, those constants shouldn't strictly be necessary to compile, and the compilation of that file can be disabled by setting the `GSSAPI_MECHS` environment variable to an empty string at build time:

https://github.com/pythongssapi/python-gssapi/blob/7018e588d36dda02e07670f305b62ce28ed4e13b/setup.py#L195-L203

> * I provided environment variables to skip detection, alternatively the detection can be enhanced to detect the mingw paths

I'd prefer to see if we can detect mingw, but an environment variable is fine for a first pass if you don't have time.

It's more pragmatic to keep the old name. Then old plugins continue to work, and there are no surprises born of clever tricks. We can rename the "distribution" (that is the PyPI name) without changing the package name; it's not at all unusual to have a difference there (though usually it's just one of capitalization).

However, if you very much want to rename the package, we can probably get away with a clever trick. Put this in the `letsencrypt` package's `__init.py__`:
```python
import sys
import certbot

sys.modules['letsencrypt'] = certbot
```
Python's import system is full of special cases, so I don't doubt that will fail in some dark corner, but all of these common uses seem to work:
```
import certbot
```
```
from certbot import whatever
```
```
from certbot.whatever import whatever_else
```

Well, ultimately this is going to happen, but I don't think everything added to cuDNN will necessarily be substantially faster than, say, vanilla Theano right from the start.

> If a combination of more primitive operations is faster, then why bother adding it to cuDNN in the first place?

Because a) those simpler primitives may be difficult enough to implement efficiently (e.g., for batch normalization, you need to be able to compute the mean and variance) and b) grouping them creates the opportunity to improve the underlying implementation in future. Most of the patches seem great but we've found a problem with the removal of wagtailcore migrations. When we run `makemigrations` it will create a migration on a custom model that depends on the deleted migration from wagtailcore, then when you run `migrate` the command will obviously fail.

We came up with a solution that we don't like. On apps.py, we change the beginning of the `ready` method to:
```
def ready(self):
        if 'makemigrations' in sys.argv:
            return
        from django.conf import settings
        ...
```
The point of this patch is:
1. We don't create migrations for translated fields
2. We create a management command to create the fields (basically we extend `sync_translation_fields` from django_modeltranslation)

**Pros:**

1. Really simple approach
2. Separation of management commands, migrations for base fields and `sync_translation_fields` for translations

**Cons:**

1. Whenever you run the `migrate` command you also have to run the `sync_translation_fields` command (maybe we can patch `migrate` to run both???)
2. You loose all the advantages of migrations

So I just spent about 8 hours on this. There was absolutely nothing wrong related to your program, the issue was just being able to execute the libc printf/putc/malloc without simprocedures.

LIST OF BUGS THAT I FOUND THAT DIDN'T MATTER:

- pyvex interpreted 128 and 256 bit constants from libvex incorrectly
- angr didn't have the InterleaveHI IROps implemented, and attempting to implement them fucks up our op parsing regex
- simgr.run and .explore didn't take kwargs
- `_IO_putc` wasn't aliased to putc
- the system brk wasn't copied from state to successor (I would have SWORN we fixed that at one point)

LIST OF THINGS THAT ACTUALLY SOLVED THIS ISSUE:

- Implementing mprotect

I've been thinking about this, and here are my two cents:

1. We generally try to stray as far away from deepcopying objects anywhere in angr, since angr objects are highly interconnected and if we accidentally deepcopy an object holding a reference to the state or the project, we'll make a copy of the entire project, which is not good.
2. There are applications in angr currently that have the same technical requirements as your request, and they solve it by using path.info/state.globals and treating the object they want to change as immutable, whenever they need to update them, they construct a new copy instead. This is the most efficient way of doing this.
3. We do definitely need a callback for "new fork being created".
4. I am very sad at your appraisal of exploration techniques as hard to use and error-prone :(

I think the thing you're describing is out of the scope of this proposal. This is supposed to be parameters that operate with the same scope as state options, controlling stuff that's built into angr. If stuff needs to be namespaced, then there can be a state plugin, or you can store in state.globals using a tuple as a key.

> **If beginTime is specified, but not endTime, then these parameters are ignored.**
>If endTime is specified, but not beginTime, then beginTime defaults to the start of the account's match history.

I think that first bullet point doesn't make sense -- instead, like you said, if `end_time` is `None` and `begin_time` is given, then setting `end_time` to "when the call is made" seems more appropriate.

We occasionally do things like this -- where we modify stuff that Riot specifies -- because we don't think it makes sense. We're often a bit leery of doing that, however, because usually Riot does a good job.

@beriberikix Thanks a lot for you comments. The implementation of the Board Agent is based on the chapter 8 “Mesh commissioning protocol” in Thread v1.1.1 specification.  I will share a slides to you.

The Border Agent mainly implement the following features:

1. Establish the Commissioning session with External Commissioner via DTLS handshake.
2. Assist the External Commissioner to complete the Commissioner Petition with the Lead by handling the messages (COMM_PET, LEAD_PET), and keep the commissioning session between the External Commissioner and Leader through processing the messages (COMM_KA, LEAD_PET)
3. Assist the External Commissioner to achieve the Commissioner Management via relaying MGMT_SET message.
4. Relay Joiner authentication messages (RLY_RX, RLY_TX) between the External Commissioner and Joiner Router

I think the quick way to learn the Border Agent functions is to refer two diagrams in Thread specification v1.1.1.

1. figure 8-1 in page 8-1 
2. figure 8-5 in page 8-29

On Oct 18, 2016, at 3:20 PM, Nick Swanson-Hysell <notifications@github.com> wrote:

The way to install PmagPy so that it is in the Python path is to use Pip install. Instructions for how to do so are found in the PmagPy cookbook here: https://earthref.org/PmagPy/cookbook/#QQ2-1-6

1.2.1 Installing PmagPy

Here we provide instructions for using pip to install PmagPy from the command line. “Pip” is a package manager that comes standard with any modern Python distribution (NB: you will have to update it to the most recent version). Pip allows you to install and uninstall pmagpy, the package with all the low-level functionality of PmagPy and pmagpy-cli which provides the PmagPy command-line-interface and has all the packages you may use to download/upload, visualize, and manipulate data.
To install using pip:

Find and open your command line.
Update pip: type on the command line:
pip install --upgrade pip
Install or update pmagpy: use the command:
pip install --upgrade pmagpy
Install or update pmagpy-cli, use the command:
pip install --upgrade pmagpy-cli 
To uninstall either, use the command: “pip uninstall pmagpy” or “pip uninstall pmagpy-cli”
If you run into trouble, use pip to uninstall both pmagpy and pmagpy-cli, then try again to install first pmagpy and then pmagpy-cli

The other way to go is to add a statement such as:
export PYTHONPATH=$PYTHONPATH:~/PmagPy 
in your .profile or .bash_profile file that points to where PmagPy is on your local machine. Since it is in your home directory you should be able to put it into your .bash_profile as I have it above and the command line programs will then be able to find the pmagpy module.

I agree that the documentation should be updated to emphasize that certbot is installing its own crontab on Ubuntu/Debian, as this feature could cause some nasty surprises. For instance, if you use plugins that automatically deploy renewed certificates, then the default crontab could cause your server to restart at an inopportune time. Or, if you have a custom way of calling the `renew` command to specify renew/deploy hooks, you could run into a situation where the default crontab runs first, renews your certificates, but never calls your deploy hooks.

I have no problem with that. In my code above, I’m expanding `right`’s range to include the NL immediately following the `*` because that’s what is easy to do with existing means, but it would be better to stop at the COMMENT.

```
( ( a ) COMMENT NL COMMENT NL * NL COMMENT NL ( b ) COMMENT NL )
 └───────────.left───────────┘    └──────────.right───────────┘
```

> in foo(a + b), we do NOT want the argument expansion to be (a + b)

Yes, that’s what I had in mind when I said “parentheses that are not part of the parent node’s syntax” in the original comment. However, in case a function is called with a single argument, that argument’s expression can be wrapped in an arbitrary number of pairs of parentheses, and only the outermost is syntactic:

```
foo ( ( ( a + b ) ) )
     └──.args[0]───┘ after expansion
```

Hi, this functionality can now be achieved in your own dataset using the new functional interface in `torchvision.transforms` i.e.

```Python
from torchvision.transforms import crop, RandomCrop
class MyDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, crop_size):
         super(Dataset, self).__init__()
         self.crop_size = crop_size

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        img = self.get_img(idx)
        target = self.get_target(idx)
        i, j, h, w = RandomCrop.get_params(img, self.crop_size)
        img = crop(img, i, j, h, w)
        target = crop(img, i, j, h, w)
        return img, target
```

My sense is that we need to remove these cases where it feels necessary to flow computed values into resource names - that is going to generally cause complications.  It may be that there is some more targeted way to fix this for `aws.serverless.Function`, which we should try to do to unblock this particular case ahead of a solution for #918.

> are there other things I need to watch out for?

Well - the apply won't run at all during preview, so the biggest concern is that some subset of your resources won't be visible as part of the preview, but then will get created/updated/deleted anyway.

I solved this by overriding the `init_app` method of `eve_sqlalchemy.SQL` which allows you to specify your own `db` as `driver` :

```
from eve import Eve
from eve.io.base import ConnectionException
from eve_sqlalchemy import SQL as _SQL
from eve_sqlalchemy.validation import ValidatorSQL

from my_models import db


class SQL(_SQL):
    # https://github.com/pyeve/eve-sqlalchemy/blob/0.5.0/eve_sqlalchemy/__init__.py#L47-L55
    def init_app(self, app):
        try:
            # FIXME: dumb double initialisation of the
            # driver because Eve sets it to None in __init__
            self.driver = db
            self.driver.app = app
            self.driver.init_app(app)
        except Exception as e:
            raise ConnectionException(e)


app = Eve(validator=ValidatorSQL, data=SQL)
```
